summary: Drawing with SKBlendMode.DstOver gradually slows down on UWP ARM64 (Surface Pro X / Microsoft SQ1), while running the same x64 build via emulation on the same device performs normally.

bugSignals.errorMessage: Drawing lines using SKBlendMode.DstOver on Windows ARM devices is very slow

stepsToReproduce: Build a UWP + Xamarin.Forms app targeting ARM64 using SkiaSharp.Views.Forms 2.88.3
Use SKBlendMode.DstOver on SKPaint stroke style
Draw multiple paths by touch on an ARM64 device (e.g. Surface Pro X / SQ1)
Observe gradual slowdown as more paths are added
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: Surface Pro X (Microsoft SQ1, ARM64), UWP, SkiaSharp.Views.Forms 2.88.3, Visual Studio 2022 17.4.1

repoLinks.description: Base demo project used to create the reproduction

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The underlying issue is in native ARM64 compilation with MSVC rather than LLVM/Clang, which has not changed between 2.88.x and later releases.

analysis.summary: The ARM64 Windows build of libSkiaSharp is compiled with MSVC, which lacks the ARM-specific SIMD/vectorization optimizations that Skia is designed for with LLVM/Clang. This explains why the ARM64-native build is slow for blend-mode-heavy operations like DstOver, while running via x64 emulation uses an optimized x64 code path and performs better.
analysis.rationale: The title says [QUESTION] but the reporter describes a real, reproducible performance degradation. The maintainer confirmed the root cause (MSVC for UWP ARM = no LLVM/Clang ARM optimizations). The build script (native/windows/build.cake) supports optional LLVM via LLVM_HOME; without it, MSVC is used for all Windows architectures including ARM64. This is a native build quality issue, not a SkiaSharp C# wrapper issue. Classified as type/bug with performance error type, in area/libSkiaSharp.native.

keySignals.text: Drawing lines using SKBlendMode.DstOver on Windows ARM devices is very slow
keySignals.interpretation: Specific blend mode triggers heavy pixel-level computation; lack of SIMD/NEON optimizations for ARM in MSVC builds makes this expensive.

keySignals.text: when I run x64 built app on an Windows ARM device it works fine
keySignals.interpretation: x64 Skia build uses LLVM/Clang with x64 SIMD optimizations; running via x64 emulation on ARM bypasses the unoptimized ARM64 code path.

keySignals.text: UWP ARM is the least optimized platform so could very well be the case. Skia is designed for LLVM/Clang, but UWP is using MSVC that has none of the optimizations.
keySignals.interpretation: Maintainer confirmed the root cause: MSVC for UWP ARM64 lacks the compiler-level ARM NEON/SIMD support that LLVM/Clang enables.

codeInvestigation.finding: The Windows ARM64 build uses MSVC by default. LLVM/Clang is optional via LLVM_HOME argument: `var clang = string.IsNullOrEmpty(LLVM_HOME.FullPath) ? "" : $"clang_win='{LLVM_HOME}'"`. If LLVM_HOME is empty, no clang_win is passed to GN, so Skia builds with MSVC which lacks ARM NEON auto-vectorization.
SKBlendMode.DstOver is a standard Porter-Duff composite mode fully supported in the C# bindings. The issue is not in the binding layer but in the native library performance.

workarounds: Build and deploy the x64 variant of the app — Windows on ARM can run x64 apps via emulation. The x64 Skia build is compiled with LLVM/Clang and is significantly faster for blend-mode operations on ARM devices.
Avoid SKBlendMode.DstOver; use SKBlendMode.SrcOver (the default) where possible, as Porter-Duff modes that require reading the destination pixel are more expensive than source-only modes.
Reduce path complexity by periodically merging drawn paths into a bitmap snapshot to reduce per-frame drawing work.
nextQuestions: Is UWP ARM64 built with clang_win in CI? Is LLVM_HOME set in the UWP build pipeline?
Would switching to clang_win for all Windows ARM64 builds be feasible given UWP toolchain constraints?
Does the issue occur with other non-SrcOver blend modes (e.g., Multiply, Screen) or is it specific to DstOver?

resolution.hypothesis: The ARM64 libSkiaSharp.dll for Windows/UWP is built with MSVC, which does not provide LLVM-style ARM NEON auto-vectorization. Enabling clang_win in the Windows ARM64 GN build would enable LLVM-style optimizations and likely fix the performance regression.

proposals.title: Use LLVM/Clang for Windows ARM64 builds
proposals.description: Modify native/windows/build.cake to always use clang_win for the ARM64 target, or ensure that the CI ARM64 build uses LLVM_HOME. This would enable ARM NEON SIMD optimizations that MSVC cannot provide.

proposals.title: Use x64 build via emulation (workaround)
proposals.description: Publish and deploy the x64 version of the app on ARM devices. Windows on ARM provides native x64 emulation. The x64 Skia build is optimized with LLVM/Clang and performs significantly better for blend-mode operations.

proposals.title: Reduce blend mode complexity
proposals.description: Switch from SKBlendMode.DstOver to SKBlendMode.SrcOver (default) where possible, or periodically flatten drawn paths into a cached bitmap to reduce per-frame computation.

resolution.recommendedReason: Immediate actionable workaround with high confidence. Allows the user to ship while the native build quality issue is tracked separately.

actionability.reason: Root cause is confirmed (MSVC vs LLVM/Clang for ARM64), repro is provided, and there is a potential fix path (enabling clang_win for ARM64 native build). Needs investigation into build toolchain feasibility before a fix can be committed.

actions.description: Correct type to type/bug (performance issue), keep area/libSkiaSharp.native and os/Windows-Universal-UWP, add tenet/performance
Acknowledge the root cause, provide workaround, and note the path to fix
actions.comment:
Thanks for providing a reproduction case and confirming the device.

The root cause is that the ARM64 native build of `libSkiaSharp` for Windows/UWP is compiled with MSVC, which does not support the ARM NEON SIMD intrinsics that Skia relies on for blend-mode operations. The x64 build is compiled with LLVM/Clang and includes those optimizations — which is why x64-via-emulation is faster on your ARM device.

**Immediate workaround:** Build and publish your app targeting `x64` rather than `ARM64`. Windows on ARM supports x64 app emulation and the x64 Skia binary is significantly faster for `SKBlendMode.DstOver` and related operations.

**Longer term:** We would need to switch the ARM64 Windows build to use LLVM/Clang (`clang_win` in the GN build) to get the same SIMD optimizations. This requires build toolchain investigation.

Alternatively, you can reduce per-frame work by periodically rasterizing completed paths into a cached bitmap, so only the active stroke needs to be re-rendered each frame.
